attributes.stem:
A 50-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a painless lesion on his tongue that he noticed while brushing his teeth. He reports being unable to scrape it off and is unsure how long it has been present. He denies dysphagia, pain, or any burning sensation of his tongue. His medical history includes HIV infection, currently well-controlled on antiretroviral therapy with a CD4+ T cell count of 550 cells/µL. He recently completed a course of antibiotics for a chest infection. He takes antiretroviral medication daily. He has a 20 pack-year smoking history, drinks about three cans of beer daily, and is a former intravenous drug user.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Oral examination reveals a white, corrugated plaque on the lateral border of his tongue that cannot be scraped off (Image).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with a white plaque on his tongue that cannot be scraped off, which is consistent with leukoplakia. Given his risk factors (smoking, alcohol consumption, and HIV status), this lesion requires thorough evaluation to rule out malignancy.

Tissue biopsy is the most appropriate next step in management. Leukoplakia is considered a premalignant condition with approximately 17% of cases progressing to squamous cell carcinoma, with a 3% annual malignant transformation rate. Any suspicious oral lesion, particularly in a patient with risk factors, must be biopsied to exclude malignancy before any treatment is initiated.

Topical betamethasone would be inappropriate without a definitive diagnosis. Corticosteroids might be used for inflammatory conditions like lichen planus but would not address potential malignancy or viral-associated lesions and could potentially mask symptoms or delay proper diagnosis.

Oral acyclovir might be considered if oral hairy leukoplakia (OHL) is strongly suspected, as OHL is associated with Epstein-Barr virus infection, particularly in immunocompromised patients. However, even in cases of suspected OHL, a biopsy should be performed first to confirm the diagnosis and rule out malignancy.

Amphotericin B lozenges and nystatin drops are both used to treat oral candidiasis (thrush). While candidiasis can present with white plaques, these typically have a more 'fluffy' appearance and can be scraped off, unlike the lesion described in this patient.

After biopsy confirms the diagnosis, management would depend on the findings. If benign leukoplakia is confirmed, smoking cessation and alcohol reduction would be recommended. If oral hairy leukoplakia is diagnosed, antiviral therapy might be considered, especially if the lesions are extensive or symptomatic. Regular follow-up is essential regardless of the diagnosis due to the risk of malignant transformation.
learningPoints: Leukoplakia is a premalignant condition with a 3% annual malignant transformation rate requiring biopsy to exclude squamous cell carcinoma.
Oral hairy leukoplakia is specifically associated with Epstein-Barr virus infection and is common in immunocompromised patients.
Risk factors for leukoplakia include tobacco use, alcohol consumption, and immunosuppression.
references: Habif T. Premalignant and malignant nonmelanoma skin tumors. Chapter 21, Clinical Dermatology:809-854.

Carrard VC, van der Waal I. A clinical diagnosis of oral leukoplakia; A guide for dentists. Med Oral Patol Oral Cir Bucal. 2018 Jan 1;23(1):e59-e64. Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5822541/
